# Questions in Livestream 107

Live Stream #107: A Hot Mess of Ebal Amulet Epigraphy

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=463" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:43</a> | Could both the `480 years` of 1 Kings 6:1 and the Ramesses reference be taken literally by dating the temple much later? | He said no; Judah-Assyria synchronisms lock Solomon's temple into the tenth century BC, so that workaround fails. | He appreciated the creative attempt, but said the evidence will not let Solomon's temple move down to around 820 BC. The synchronisms between Judah's dynasty and Assyria force a tenth-century date around 966 or 967 BC, so interpreters still have to decide how to handle either the 480 years or the Ramesses reference rather than harmonizing them by redating the temple. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:52</a> | How did ancient Egyptian form the past tense? | He said Middle Egyptian typically marks it by inserting `n` into the verbal form before the suffix. | He described the regular grammar using the verb `sDm`, noting that the past-tense form in Middle Egyptian is the `sDm.n=f`: an `n` is inserted between the verbal root and pronominal suffix. He cautioned that this is fully visible in Middle Egyptian, while archaic or Old Egyptian does not show the same form in the same way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=749" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:29</a> | With groups such as ABR making Christian academia look like a laughingstock, are there reputable bodies supporting solid evidence-based work? | He said ABR took over NEAS and drove moderates elsewhere, including IBR and discipline-specific groups, but no equivalent moderate replacement has really filled NEAS's former role. | He said NEAS had once been a more broadly evangelical archaeology society, but ABR's takeover drove many moderate or more academically minded people into IBR or narrower disciplinary organizations. In his view, bad archaeology pushed out good archaeology, and the result has been polarization rather than a clean replacement for NEAS's former role. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=931" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:31</a> | Do you think ABR will ever do that to ETS? | He said no; ETS is too large for ABR to take over, though ABR has influenced it through NEAS activity and sympathetic journal connections. | He answered that ABR is far too small to capture ETS the way it captured NEAS, because ETS has thousands of members rather than dozens. Still, he said ABR has influence inside ETS through the NEAS conference presence and through sympathetic journal channels, so takeover is unlikely but influence is real. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=1059" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:39</a> | Are there Egyptian monotheistic cult sites that parallel double altars or double standing stones? | He said yes; Amarna/Akhetaten gives examples of multiple altars, and such installations can have different ritual purposes. | He pointed to Amarna/Akhetaten as an Egyptian monotheistic cult setting with multiple altar sites. He added that multiple altars do not necessarily mean duplicate offerings; one altar might be for burnt offerings, another for incense or libations, and standing stones could likewise appear in multiples for ordinary cultic reasons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=1207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:07</a> | Why was there still a redemption requirement for Israel's firstborn after the tenth plague? | He said the plague sparing and the later redemption rite serve different purposes, and `firstborn` is about heir status, not just newborn babies. | He warned that limiting the census issue to newborn males misunderstands `firstborn` in an ancient Near Eastern setting, where it refers to heir status and can apply even when the person is elderly. He also distinguished the Passover sparing, which marked households from divine judgment, from later redemption fees and firstborn rites, which had different ritual and communal functions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=1491" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:51</a> | Does using cultural context to understand scripture create relativism? | He said the opposite: cultural context helps clarify what the text meant instead of undermining it. | He argued that every interpretation uses some context, so the real choice is whether to use the ancient context of the original audience or a modern context that shifts with contemporary assumptions. In his view, authorial intent and ancient cultural setting restrain relativism, while reading the Bible by what it means to people today is what produces unstable meanings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=1887" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:27</a> | In Numbers, why did God require sacrifices when people were starving, and how did they survive? | He said most sacrifices were not as onerous as assumed, many fed families or Levites, and Deuteronomy frames obedience with promised provision. | He said the sacrificial system should not be imagined as constant ruinous loss: many offerings were eaten by families or supported the Levites, who had no land inheritance, and the tithe functioned like a religious tax. He also placed the question inside Deuteronomy's blessings-and-curses framework, where Israel's obedience is tied to promised agricultural provision. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=2308" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:28</a> | In Numbers 25:8, was Phinehas's stabbing symbolic or literal, and why were both people killed the same way? | He said the Hebrew depicts a literal, graphic act in which Phinehas pierced both offenders, stopping a plague tied to blatant communal sin. | He treated the passage as deliberately graphic rather than symbolic. The sin was public and affected the whole community, so Phinehas's act is presented as a literal intervention against blatant communal disobedience, with both participants killed because both were involved in the offense that had brought judgment on Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=2725" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:25</a> | What are the holy tools in Numbers 31:6, and did Israel have holy war tools? | He said they were Tabernacle implements, not weapons, though captured enemy weapons could sometimes become symbols of God's deliverance. | He identified the `holy vessels` as Tabernacle equipment such as the ark, altars, offering table, incense altar, and related ritual furniture carried in connection with war to seek God's blessing. He distinguished those from weapons, while allowing that a defeated enemy's weapon, such as Goliath's sword, could later be kept as a sign of God's deliverance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=3022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:22</a> | Was there a difference between murder and murder with a weapon, and why did the high priest need to die before returning? | He was unsure about the high-priest part, but said weapon language marks intent and means, while refuge cities handled accidental killings. | He redirected the passage to Numbers and the cities of refuge, where an accused killer could flee from vengeance if the death was accidental or the accusation false. The weapon language helps identify intent and manner of killing; he did not claim to understand the high-priest death question fully, but kept the main distinction on accidental killing versus murder. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=3496" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:16</a> | Is there a good Bible commentary covering almost every Old Testament verse, and what are good fantasy books or thoughts on The Witcher? | He said no single good commentary covers the whole Old Testament; use strong book-specific commentaries, and he mentioned Tolkien, Shadowrun, and limited experience with The Witcher. | He said quality Old Testament commentary work is usually book-specific, so a single nearly verse-by-verse volume set will not be uniformly strong across the canon. On the fantasy side, he mentioned Tolkien positively, referred to Shadowrun, and said he had only limited exposure to The Witcher rather than offering a detailed judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=4002" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:42</a> | If the Father and Son are both God, why does Jesus seem to deny eye-for-eye and tooth-for-tooth rules? | He said Father and Son are distinct persons of the Trinity, and Jesus was not denying civil justice but adding mercy and grace to personal conduct. | He framed the Father and Son as distinct persons within the Trinity rather than the same person contradicting himself. He also distinguished civil justice from personal retaliation: Jesus is not abolishing courts or proportional justice, but teaching disciples not to make personal vengeance the guiding principle of their conduct. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=4266" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:06</a> | Is there a good book that reconciles the God of the Old Testament with the God of the New Testament? | He mainly warned that outright denial of the Old Testament is a Marcionite error and urged listeners toward readings that keep the full canon together. | Rather than centering one title, he emphasized that Christians should not resolve the tension by rejecting the Old Testament or treating its God as different from the New Testament God. He described that move as Marcionite and pushed instead toward interpretation that keeps both Testaments within the same canon and theological story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=4603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:43</a> | Did Gershon Galil's high-resolution image change the Mount Ebal tablet debate? | He said the improved image was welcome, but he still did not see the claimed inscription clearly enough to change his view. | He said better imaging is always welcome and can be useful where writing is clear, but the Ebal amulet remained too degraded for him to see the claimed inscription. His objection was not to imaging as a method but to deriving epigraphy from an image that, in his judgment, does not justify the proposed reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=4944" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:24</a> | What is missing from history presented for a general audience, and what is scholarship often in denial about? | He said scholarship often lacks respect for texts, while popular history often misses cultural context, material culture, props, and costuming details. | He contrasted academic and popular failures: scholarship can become too dismissive of texts, while public history often neglects the lived cultural setting, objects, clothing, props, and material details that make ancient life intelligible. His answer stressed that both texts and material culture need to be handled seriously. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=5414" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:14</a> | Could some Greek, Roman, Egyptian, or Indian gods be real, or could some be sons of God? | He said there may be demonic influence or legendary human figures behind some gods, but he was cautious about tying them directly to Genesis 6 sons of God. | He allowed that some pagan deities might reflect demonic influence, mythologized human figures, or other spiritual realities, but he did not want to flatten all such figures into one category. In particular, he was cautious about directly identifying them with Genesis 6 sons of God without stronger evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=5632" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:52</a> | Did Hammurabi campaign into the Levant, and what do secular scholars think about Amraphel being Hammurabi? | He said Hammurabi reached Syria/the Levant, but whether he went as far south as Sodom and Gomorrah is uncertain; many scholars reject the Amraphel link on chronological grounds. | He said Hammurabi did campaign into Syria and the broader Levantine sphere, but he was not ready to say that meant he reached the southern region connected with Sodom and Gomorrah. He also noted that many scholars reject identifying Amraphel with Hammurabi because the chronologies do not line up for them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=5969" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:29</a> | Is it too far to use `church` in Matthew 16:18 as a public-space argument for Christians in politics? | He said yes for that passage, though Christians may serve publicly under kingdom ethics without forcing religious law or privileging Christians coercively. | He thought Matthew 16:18 is being stretched too far if it is made into a direct argument for Christian political control of public space. He still allowed Christians to participate in public office, but said they should do so under kingdom ethics, not by coercing religious law or demanding special treatment for Christians. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=6207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:27</a> | In Zechariah 9:9, what does the Hebrew word often rendered `salvation` mean? | He said the form is best understood as `victorious` in that context, because the verbal construction points to one able to help himself. | He argued that the Hebrew form is not best read as passively `saved` in this context, but as `victorious` or able to bring help. He connected that to the grammar of the verbal construction and to the messianic picture of a righteous king who comes humbly but not helplessly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=6482" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:02</a> | Does the Song of Deborah indicate Yahweh was originally a Midianite war god? | He said the Midianite/Kenite hypothesis is plausible from Moses traditions, but the evidence is limited and the exact nature of Yahweh in Midian remains uncertain. | He treated the Midianite or Kenite connection as a plausible historical hypothesis because Moses has strong Midianite links, but he would not turn it into certainty. The evidence may suggest Yahweh was known in that region, yet it does not clearly establish exactly what sort of deity Midianites understood Yahweh to be or how that relates to Israel's later theology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=6800" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:20</a> | Why would King Abimelech be interested in marrying Sarah given her age? | He argued the patriarchal ages should not be read naively here, and the Sarah figure in the episode was likely much younger; a political angle is possible but not decisive. | He said the narrative becomes difficult only if the patriarchal ages are read in a flatly literal modern way at that point. He suggested Sarah in the episode should be imagined as younger than the surface chronology implies, while also allowing that marriage could have had political value, though he did not make that the decisive explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=7236" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:36</a> | Were Shu, Tefnut, Geb, and other Egyptian gods thought to have human-shaped material bodies, or was that just iconography? | He said Egyptian gods could have both a natural substance and an anthropomorphic body, so the images were not merely decorative iconography. | He said Egyptian divine representation was not merely a symbolic art convention. Gods could be associated with a natural manifestation or substance while also possessing anthropomorphic embodiment, so their human-shaped depictions participated in how Egyptians conceptualized divine bodies rather than functioning as decoration only. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=7499" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:59</a> | What should be said about Black Hebrew Israelites? | He described it as a recent African-American religious movement blending Afrocentrism and biblical claims, and said its historical arguments do not hold up. | He described Black Hebrew Israelite claims as a modern movement rather than a historically established identity, blending Afrocentric arguments with biblical claims about Israel. His criticism was historical: the movement's claims about ancestry and ancient evidence do not stand up when checked against the sources. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=7801" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:01</a> | Why do Christians not believe in oral Torah? | He said Christians do not treat the Talmud or oral Torah as authoritative because much of it is late, speculative, and unreliable for original context, though it can still be read with value. | He distinguished usefulness from authority. Christians can read rabbinic material for insight, but he said oral Torah and the Talmud are late, contain speculation, and cannot be treated as binding revelation or as straightforward evidence for the original biblical context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=8243" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:23</a> | From a biblical perspective, why did God allow other religions to develop, and why has he not intervened recently? | He said counterfeit religion implies a genuine one, and used the wheat-and-tares parable to argue that judgment is delayed to avoid harming those who may yet come to Christ. | He reasoned that false religion is parasitic on the existence of true worship, then turned to Jesus' wheat-and-tares parable to explain delay. In that framing, God postpones final judgment because immediate removal of evil would also harm people who may yet come to repentance and faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=8563" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:43</a> | Are you more of an Old Testament or New Testament guy? | He said he is more drawn to the Old Testament because it is less worked over, spans more time and context, and remains essential for understanding the New Testament. | He said his interests lean Old Testament because it covers a broader sweep of time, cultures, languages, and historical settings, and because many of its questions remain less exhausted by scholarship. He also stressed that the New Testament depends heavily on Old Testament context, so the older material is indispensable for reading the later canon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=8855" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:35</a> | What is your view of heaven, hell, and purgatory? | He rejected purgatory, affirmed heaven as eternal life and bliss with God, and deferred a fuller explanation of hell to a later show. | He rejected purgatory and affirmed heaven in terms of eternal life and blessed communion with God. He did not give a full doctrine of hell in this answer, instead saying that subject needed a dedicated treatment later rather than a quick answer in this stream. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=9071" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:11</a> | Why did God choose Moses and the prophets, and what criteria did he use? | He said prophets are servants and intermediaries whose main work is often forth-telling the present clearly, and God uses fallible people for that task. | He said prophecy is not mainly prediction but forth-telling: seeing and declaring the present truth clearly. Moses becomes the model because Israel could not endure direct divine speech, so prophets serve as intermediaries and messengers, even though figures like Jonah and Balaam show that God can use unwilling or morally flawed people. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=9563" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:23</a> | If the Torah was edited during the exile to counter Marduk and Babylon, why prefer a fallen-priest interpretation over the apkallu? | He said he does not accept exile-era reshaping of the Torah in that way, favors earlier core documents with later stitching or updating, and sees the apkallu tradition as a poor fit. | He rejected the premise that the Torah was substantially reshaped in exile to answer Babylonian material, pointing instead to pre-exilic Torah excerpts and a supplementary model with earlier core documents stitched and updated by editors. Because he dates the core material earlier, he prefers the fallen-priest reading and thinks the apkallu comparison is chronologically and conceptually strained. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=9948" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:48</a> | What resource or commentary on John would you recommend for a church class? | He recommended D. A. Carson's commentary on John. | He recommended D. A. Carson's commentary as his preferred resource for John, especially for a class that moves through the Gospel exegetically while following theological and apologetic trails. He thought Carson was a reliable expert on John and said the commentary would be the place he would go. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=10444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:04</a> | What about the claim that Jesus lied about prayer and faith because literal mountains do not move and believers do not receive everything asked in his name? | He said moving mountains is figurative, not a failed literal promise, and prayer also assumes God's will, goodness, and timing. | He said the mountain-moving language was never meant as a literal engineering claim, so treating it as a failed promise imposes a flat literalism on figurative speech. On prayer, he argued that God's answers include no, wait, or not now, and that requests in Jesus' name still operate under God's will, goodness, and purposes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=11005" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:25</a> | Does the wheat-and-tares answer also apply to where Jesus stops when quoting Isaiah 61:1-2 in Luke 4:17-21? | He said yes in broad pattern: Luke 4 leaves the day of vengeance for later, and the wheat-and-tares theme amplifies delayed judgment. | He compared Luke 4's quotation of Isaiah 61 with the omitted line about the day of vengeance, saying the pattern fits delayed judgment. The wheat-and-tares parable is not identical to Luke 4, but it amplifies the same broad theme that mercy and proclamation precede final separation and vengeance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=11306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:26</a> | Who was the earliest known Egyptian king, and what do we know about him? | He said Narmer or Hor-Aha are candidates for the earliest unified king, while the earliest recorded royal name fragment on the Palermo Stone may read `Poo`. | He distinguished the earliest unified king from the earliest recorded royal name: Narmer and Hor-Aha are the main candidates around Dynasty 0 and Dynasty 1, but earlier royal names appear in fragmentary form. Looking to Beckerath and the Palermo Stone, he said the earliest fragment he could cite is written with signs that may read `Poo`, while warning that it is probably not a complete name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=11617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:37</a> | Do you recommend K. A. Kitchen's `On the Reliability of the Old Testament`? | He said yes; it is dated and needs a second edition, but remains valuable and has not really been replaced. | He strongly recommended Kitchen's book, calling it large, still useful, and not really replaced, even though some archaeology is now dated and a second edition would be helpful. He also disclosed his own connection to Kitchen as a former research assistant and mentor, especially for chronology and epigraphy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=12004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:04</a> | How are ancient sites identified, such as Troy or Jericho? | He said identifications combine geography, texts, archaeology, continuity of local names, inscriptions, archives, and informed inference, with Egyptian sites often easier when labels survive. | He said identifications depend on the site and discipline: Troy is argued from geography, access, and archaeological fit with the narrative, while Jericho benefits from location, tell continuity, and long-standing local identification. Egyptian sites can be easier when statue bases or inscriptions name kings or places, but even then archaeologists make informed inferences that can be revised. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=12590" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:50</a> | Is the Ipuwer Papyrus good evidence for the Exodus plagues? | He said it is not direct evidence because it is a Middle Kingdom text, but later copying clusters may suggest Egyptians used old prophetic literature to process crisis memories. | He said Ipuwer is not a direct record of the Exodus plagues because its grammar, internal evidence, and setting point to the Middle Kingdom, centuries too early. Its value is indirect: it shows Egyptians knew literary descriptions of social and natural catastrophe, and later copying clusters may reveal how Egyptians reused such texts during later crises. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=13049" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:29</a> | How would you critique the idea that all religions are rivers flowing into the same ocean and lead to the same God? | He rejected it because religions make contradictory claims about God's attributes, and he appealed to Christ as the exclusive way to the Father. | He rejected the metaphor because religions define God with incompatible attributes: Christianity's triune and good God, Islam's strict monotheism as he described it, and Hindu pantheistic or polytheistic frameworks do not point to the same destination. He then grounded his Christian critique in Jesus' exclusive claim to be the way, truth, and life. |
